other_observers: Brandon Percival, Duane Nelson, Stan Oswald; I found and identified the bird
duration: off and on for about 20 minutes
specific_location: Pueblo Res. at various locations: at east end, south side with 100s of other gulls chasing ducks (11:00); at east end, north side with 100s of roosting and bathing gulls (13:05); on tire jetty at South Marina (15:00)
description: While sorting through swimming and flying gulls chasing ducks around, I noted a very pale gull flying. It was obviously pale enough that it had to be either an Iceland Gull (ICGU) or a Glaucous-winged Gull (GWGU); in my mind, it was obviously not a Glaucous Gull (GLGU). When it landed on the water, it became obvious that it was a pale-end ICGU.  The whole plumage was whitish with individual feathers having variably brown markings, but with the overall impression of a very pale gull. The head was somewhat rounded, but large and blocky enough that I suspect that the bird was a male; it certainly wasn't the very dove-like head of a very small, thus female, ICGU. The bill was shorter and thinner than those of the nearby Herring Gulls (HERG) and nearly entirely black. The eyes were dark and the legs were bright pink.  The wings were quite long, with the wingtip projection longer than that of nearby HERGs. The outer primaries were the whitest part of the plumage, with only barely-darker subterminal anchors noticeable at short distance. The bird rode high in the water, with the back angled up from the neck to the wingtips; not as strongly angled as on the Ring-billed Gulls, but more obviously so than the nearby HERGs.Behaviors: Flying; swimming; chasing ducks with fish; standing and roosting on tiresCall: None heardPlumage: Juvenal
